VIVERSE: Platform Assessment & Strategic Opportunity

A browser-based content platform built by HTC. No install required. 1 million monthly active users and a creator economy that pays for real engagement, not ad impressions.

Partner Program
Engagement-based payouts tied to unique views and time spent. No follower minimum required.
Subscriptions & Purchases
Direct creator-to-audience monetization. 80/20 revenue split in the creator's favor.
Closed beta
Creator Grants
Funded development contracts for pitched projects. Support from concept through launch.
Read the assessment ↓
02 — Competitive landscape

Three points of comparison

The browser-based content market includes many players. Three are most instructive as comparisons given their scale, openness to indie creators, and relevance to the web content space. This is not an exhaustive market map.

Platform Scale Model Creator control
Poki Casual web gaming leader ~100M MAU Ad revenue share. Curator-driven. Built almost entirely on organic search. Platform owns the audience and approves all content. Low. Platform controls discovery, content approval, and monetization format.
CrazyGames Casual web gaming ~35–40M MAU Ad revenue share. Curator-driven. Proprietary SDK required for monetization. Quality gate on all submissions. Low. Requires proprietary toolchain and platform approval before anything goes live.
itch.io Indie marketplace ~30M visits* Open marketplace. Creator sets price and revenue share. Has browse, tags, jams, and community features but discovery is creator-driven, not platform-curated. High. But the audience belongs to the creator, not the platform.

* itch.io does not publish official MAU figures. ~30M reflects estimated monthly web traffic from secondary analytics sources and is not equivalent to audited MAU. CrazyGames officially reported 35M MAU in mid-2025; current figure reflects continued growth.

03 — Positioning

The third lane

VIVERSE is not competing in any of those lanes. That is the point.

The existing lanes

Poki and CrazyGames are curated game portals. Content performs similarly across both. The market is established, the players are entrenched, and Poki's organic search moat took a decade to build. That race is over before it starts.

itch.io is a store, not a destination platform. Discovery is creator-driven. Traffic finds content through search and community, not algorithmic surfacing. The audience belongs to the creator, not the platform.

VIVERSE

An open web publishing platform where creators own their IP, use the tools they already know, get paid for real engagement rather than ad impressions, and can embed their content anywhere on the open web.

Critically, VIVERSE also has something none of the comparison platforms offer: spatial community infrastructure. Avatars, shared 3D worlds, multiplayer spaces, and persistent social environments built natively into the platform.

04 — The real opportunity

Content that cannot exist elsewhere

Poki and CrazyGames host casual browser games. Fun, frictionless, disposable. Five minutes and you close the tab. VIVERSE hosts a category those platforms are not built for and do not surface.

In Tirol
A fully realized immersive 3D world — a virtual recreation of the Austrian Alps navigable in a browser. Not a game. A destination.
Frustrain
An award-winning XR experience from a professional film director. The line between game, film, and spatial environment deliberately blurred.
The Obscura Society
Built by Atlas Obscura. They evaluated Meta's Horizon Worlds and chose VIVERSE for its lower barrier to entry and open-web embeddability.
The open market

There is no dominant open web platform for immersive WebXR experiences. There is no Poki for this content category. No other open web platform is optimized to surface, monetize, or build community around it. That market is open.

05 — Competitive context

What about Meta?

Meta is the one player that warrants a closer look before assessing VIVERSE's position in the immersive web space.

What Meta is building

Meta's open-source Immersive Web SDK is built on Three.js, supports AI-assisted development, and is available under an MIT license. Meta has referenced growth in browser-based WebXR usage through the Quest browser, though specific MAU figures have not been independently verified. That investment is real and cannot be dismissed.

Horizon Worlds has retrenched specifically on VR. In early 2026, Meta separated Worlds from the Quest VR platform, shifted focus to mobile, and announced no new games would be added to the VR version. This is a retreat from immersive social worlds, not from VR broadly.

The key distinction

Meta is building tools, not a platform. Their SDK helps creators build WebXR experiences but does not provide a destination to publish to, an audience to reach, a monetization system to earn from, or a community to belong to.

It is a development framework. VIVERSE is a creator economy. Atlas Obscura evaluated both and chose VIVERSE. That is a signal from an organization that did the comparison.

The defensible claim

No direct competitor has been identified with the same combination of engine-agnostic publishing, engagement-based creator monetization, IP ownership guarantees, and spatial community infrastructure in a single open-web platform. Meta is building WebXR tooling. Nobody appears to be building what VIVERSE is building as a complete package.

06 — Evidence

Proven vs. not yet proven

Honest accounting of what the research actually supports versus what remains undemonstrated.

What the evidence supports

Web gaming scales to large audiences via browser. Poki's 2026 State of Web Gaming report shows average web gamers spend 11 to 20 minutes per session engaging with multiple titles. The no-install browser model is proven to reach console-scale audiences. Industry analysts and financial media have increasingly cited browser gaming as one of the fastest-growing segments in games. Atlas Obscura chose VIVERSE over Horizon Worlds in a direct evaluation.

What is not yet proven

Whether immersive WebXR experiences specifically can drive tens of millions of MAU. No platform has demonstrated that. VIVERSE at 1M MAU is a starting point, not a proof of trajectory. The content category is harder to produce than casual games. The Poki session data reflects casual game behavior and should not be assumed to transfer directly to 3D immersive experiences. Meta has spent billions on immersive demand without cracking it at scale.

07 — Strategic verdict

The honest verdict

The competitive window is real and it is open right now. VIVERSE appears to be the only destination on the open web combining immersive content, creator ownership, engagement-based monetization, and spatial community in one place. The no-install model removes the biggest historical barrier to this type of experience. The spatial community layer, if built deliberately, becomes a moat that no casual game portal and no developer SDK can replicate.

The path to tens of millions of MAU requires one or more of three things:

1
A breakout piece of content that pulls a mass audience in and establishes the category in the public imagination.
2
A platform-level discovery engine that does not yet exist — the equivalent of Poki's organic search moat, built for immersive content.
3
A broader cultural shift toward immersive web experiences that is still early but directionally underway.
Strategic direction

Do not compete with Poki on Poki's terms or with Meta on Meta's budget. Own the category that has no clear winner yet, build the community around it, and establish the identity through content quality before anyone else gets there.
